description:
You have just connected to a freshly-started Linux workstation whose system clock is already set to UTC.  
Your colleague in New-York sent two application log entries that are time-stamped in the server’s local zone (America/New_York).  
Before you can continue debugging you must:

1. Standardise the shell’s locale so that every tool produces UTF-8 output.  
2. Guarantee that any subsequent calls to “date -u” return values in UTC, regardless of the workstation’s default settings.  
3. Convert the two given New-York timestamps to their exact, *absolute* UTC equivalents.  
4. Produce a small, machine-readable verification log so that the QA robot can grade your work later.

IMPORTANT – directory layout & file formats (strictly follow these rules)

A. Directory  
   • All artefacts must live inside /home/user/timezone_debug  
     (create it if it does not already exist).

B. Environment snapshot  
   • File: /home/user/timezone_debug/env_snapshot.log  
   • Content: exactly TWO lines (no leading or trailing blanks).  
       Line 1: LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8  
       Line 2: TZ=UTC  

C. Conversion log  
   • File: /home/user/timezone_debug/tz_conversion.log  
   • Content: exactly TWO lines, one per original timestamp.  
   • Line order must match the order presented below.  
   • Format of every line (no spaces around the semicolon):  
       ORIGINAL=<YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS America/New_York>;UTC=<YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS UTC>  

   • Required lines (copy these *verbatim* after you have calculated the UTC values):  
       1) ORIGINAL=2023-05-15 14:23:55 America/New_York;UTC=2023-05-15 18:23:55 UTC  
       2) ORIGINAL=2024-01-31 07:00:00 America/New_York;UTC=2024-01-31 12:00:00 UTC  

D. Permissions  
   • All new files must be readable by everyone (mode 644) but writable only by you (the current non-root user).  

What you must do (typical action count: 5-10 commands)

1. Export the requested locale (LC_ALL) and timezone (TZ) variables *for the current shell session*.  
2. Verify that they really took effect (hint: “locale” and “echo $TZ”).  
3. Calculate the UTC equivalents of the two New-York timestamps (you may use “date -d … -u” or any other POSIX-compliant method).  
4. Create /home/user/timezone_debug and write both required log files with the exact contents and permissions described above.  
5. Double-check that the formatting (including capitalisation, spacing, semicolons and line order) is 100 % identical to the specification – the automated grader is unforgiving.

When everything looks correct, you are done. The test-suite will:

• Read /home/user/timezone_debug/env_snapshot.log and compare it byte-for-byte to the reference.  
• Read /home/user/timezone_debug/tz_conversion.log and compare it byte-for-byte to the reference.  
• Inspect file permissions.

If any character or permission bit is wrong the task will fail, so pay close attention to detail.
truth:
Expected final objects (and their exact contents):

1. Directory  
   Path: /home/user/timezone_debug  
   Permissions: 755

2. File  
   Path: /home/user/timezone_debug/env_snapshot.log  
   Permissions: 644  
   Size: 28 bytes  
   SHA256: 7360d75b74501a1e83ec52e306c8b1f2b0c5ca6458e9216ce24239888904aa95  
   Contents (visible \n = newline):  
     LC_ALL=en_US.UTF-8\nTZ=UTC\n

3. File  
   Path: /home/user/timezone_debug/tz_conversion.log  
   Permissions: 644  
   Size: 137 bytes  
   SHA256: 0d734b681ab4f63a936d0b05477388568daa0ecff5459b32b4030d0966216e12  
   Contents (visible \n = newline):  
     ORIGINAL=2023-05-15 14:23:55 America/New_York;UTC=2023-05-15 18:23:55 UTC\nORIGINAL=2024-01-31 07:00:00 America/New_York;UTC=2024-01-31 12:00:00 UTC\n
name: task_000000_b2d5ca01
